executive_brief:
The threat landscape has fundamentally shifted this week, defined by a collapse in the traditional vulnerability lifecycle and the weaponization of AI against itself. State-sponsored actors, particularly from North Korea and Iran, are no longer content with espionage; they are executing financial heists and physical disruptions at unprecedented speeds. The $285 million theft from Drift Protocol [7] and the simultaneous compromise of the Axios npm package [1] signal a coordinated assault on developer trust and financial infrastructure. Simultaneously, the 'zero-day window' has evaporated. Vulnerabilities in Flowise [127], Marimo [329], and Fortinet [22] were exploited within hours of disclosure, rendering traditional patch management obsolete. 

What keeps security leaders awake tonight is the convergence of these vectors: AI tools like Anthropic's Mythos are discovering thousands of zero-days [196], while attackers use AI agents to exploit them [100]. Critical infrastructure is no longer a theoretical target; Iranian actors have actively manipulated US PLCs to disrupt operations [205], and ransomware has knocked Dutch healthcare systems offline [349]. The era of 'detect and respond' is over. We are now in the era of 'prevent or perish,' where supply chain integrity and AI governance are the primary lines of defense.

threat_stories.headline: Operation TrueChaos: DPRK's $285M Heist and the Supply Chain Purge
threat_stories.narrative:
The theft of $285 million from the Drift Protocol is not an isolated incident but the financial capstone of a broader North Korean campaign targeting the software supply chain. While the Drift heist utilized a sophisticated six-month social engineering operation involving fake trading firms [42], the UNC1069 group (linked to Lazarus) simultaneously compromised the Axios npm package [1]. This dual-pronged attack demonstrates a strategic pivot: draining crypto liquidity while poisoning the very libraries developers use to build the next generation of financial tools. 

The Axios compromise involved hijacking the maintainer account to push malicious versions enabling dependency injection [24]. This mirrors the Trivy supply chain attack on the European Commission, where attackers manipulated version tags in GitHub Actions to steal cloud credentials [17]. The pattern is clear: attackers are targeting the 'trust infrastructure' of development. By compromising maintainers and CI/CD pipelines, they bypass perimeter defenses entirely. The Drift heist drained funds in 10 seconds [71], but the Axios attack threatens every downstream application relying on the package, creating a silent, pervasive risk across the global tech ecosystem.

timeline.event: North Korean hackers drain $285M from Drift Protocol via social engineering
timeline.significance: Demonstrates high-value financial targeting with operational security

timeline.event: Axios npm package maintainer account compromised via fake LinkedIn/Slack profiles
timeline.significance: Supply chain attack vector against critical developer tooling
Confirms sustained campaign against open-source ecosystem

timeline.event: Discovery of 1,700 malicious packages across npm, PyPI, Go, Rust
timeline.significance: Scale of supply chain compromise is systemic, not isolated

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. Financial loss is immediate ($285M), but the supply chain compromise affects millions of downstream applications globally. Trust in open-source repositories is severely degraded.
threat_stories.action_required: Audit all npm dependencies immediately. Pin versions to SHA-1 hashes. Block execution of unsigned packages. Monitor for anomalous network traffic from development environments to GitHub APIs.

threat_stories.headline: The AI Arms Race: Mythos vs. The Agentic Kill Chain
threat_stories.narrative:
We are witnessing the first true AI-on-AI conflict. On one side, Anthropic's 'Project Glasswing' and the Claude Mythos model have autonomously discovered thousands of zero-day vulnerabilities across major vendors [196]. This defensive capability is a breakthrough, but it has a dark twin: attackers are weaponizing AI agents to exploit these same systems. The 'GrafanaGhost' attack [146] and the Flowise RCE [127] demonstrate that AI agents, designed to automate workflows, are becoming the primary attack surface. 

In the Flowise incident, attackers exploited a code injection flaw in the CustomMCP node to execute arbitrary code with full Node.js privileges [180]. This wasn't just a bug; it was an 'agentic' compromise where the tool itself was turned against the user. Simultaneously, LLM-generated passwords are proving indefensible, with AI agents embedding weak credentials into production code [208]. The timeline for exploitation has collapsed; the Marimo vulnerability was exploited within 10 hours of disclosure [329]. Defenders can no longer rely on vendor patch cycles; they must assume AI agents will find and weaponize flaws faster than humans can patch them.

timeline.event: Anthropic reveals Claude Mythos found thousands of zero-days
timeline.significance: AI-driven vulnerability discovery scales beyond human capacity

timeline.event: Flowise AI Agent Builder RCE (CVE-2025-59528) exploited in the wild
timeline.significance: AI development tools become primary attack vectors

timeline.event: Marimo RCE flaw exploited within 10 hours of disclosure
timeline.significance: Patch windows have effectively collapsed to zero

timeline.event: GrafanaGhost zero-click data exfiltration via AI dashboards
timeline.significance: Indirect prompt injection enables stealthy data theft

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. AI tools are both the discovery mechanism for defenders and the exploitation vector for attackers. The speed of weaponization outpaces traditional patch management.
threat_stories.action_required: Disable CustomMCP nodes in Flowise immediately. Audit all AI-generated code for hardcoded secrets. Implement strict egress controls for AI agents. Monitor for prompt injection patterns in logs.

threat_stories.headline: Critical Infrastructure Breach: Iran Targets US PLCs
threat_stories.narrative:
The threat to critical infrastructure has moved from theoretical to kinetic. Iranian-affiliated actors, including CyberAv3ngers, have successfully compromised internet-exposed Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) at US critical infrastructure sites [193]. Unlike typical cyberattacks that steal data, these actors manipulated SCADA displays and altered operational data, causing real-world disruption [205]. The attacks targeted Rockwell Automation and Siemens PLCs, exploiting default configurations and lack of segmentation. 

This campaign coincides with a massive ransomware attack on ChipSoft, a Dutch healthcare provider, which knocked EHR services offline across hospitals in the Netherlands and Belgium [349]. The convergence of state-sponsored physical disruption and criminal ransomware targeting healthcare suggests a coordinated effort to destabilize essential services. The FBI and CISA have issued urgent advisories, noting that nearly 4,000 US industrial devices remain exposed to these attacks [347]. The use of legitimate configuration tools like Studio 5000 Logix Designer makes detection nearly impossible for traditional security tools.

timeline.event: CISA/FBI warn of Iran targeting US critical infrastructure PLCs
timeline.significance: Official government acknowledgment of active OT threat

timeline.event: ChipSoft ransomware attack disrupts Dutch/Belgian healthcare
timeline.significance: Healthcare infrastructure becomes a primary target for disruption

timeline.event: Confirmation of real-world disruption at US infra sites
timeline.significance: Physical impact confirmed, not just data theft

timeline.event: Analysis reveals 4,000+ US industrial devices exposed
timeline.significance: Scale of exposure is massive and systemic

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. Physical safety and operational continuity of energy, water, and healthcare systems are at risk. Traditional IT security controls are ineffective in OT environments.
threat_stories.action_required: Isolate all OT devices from the public internet immediately. Disable remote management on PLCs. Block traffic on OT ports (502, 22, 44818). Conduct physical audits of network segmentation.

threat_stories.headline: The Zero-Day Collapse: Fortinet, Ivanti, and Adobe
threat_stories.narrative:
The window for patching critical vulnerabilities has collapsed to hours. This week saw a barrage of zero-days exploited in the wild before patches could be widely deployed. Fortinet's EMS vulnerability (CVE-2026-35616) was actively exploited, forcing CISA to order federal agencies to patch by Friday [99]. Simultaneously, Ivanti's Endpoint Manager Mobile flaw (CVE-2026-1340) was added to the KEV catalog with a four-day remediation deadline [240]. 

Adobe Reader remains a persistent threat vector, with a zero-day exploited via malicious PDFs since December 2025 [259]. Attackers are using these PDFs to fingerprint targets and exfiltrate data without user interaction [293]. The 'BlueHammer' Windows zero-day, leaked by a disgruntled researcher, allows local privilege escalation to SYSTEM [120]. This wave of zero-days indicates that vulnerability discovery is now automated and scalable. The 'patch Tuesday' model is dead; organizations must assume any unpatched system is already compromised.

timeline.event: Fortinet releases emergency patch for exploited EMS zero-day
timeline.significance: Active exploitation confirmed in the wild

timeline.event: CISA orders federal patching of Fortinet and Ivanti flaws
BlueHammer Windows zero-day exploit code leaked
timeline.significance: Privilege escalation risk for all Windows systems

timeline.event: Adobe Reader zero-day confirmed active since December

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. Unpatched systems are effectively owned by attackers. The speed of exploitation requires automated response capabilities.
threat_stories.action_required: Apply Fortinet hotfix immediately. Disable Adobe JavaScript. Rotate all credentials on Ivanti systems. Monitor for BlueHammer indicators (SAM database access).

actor_profiles.name: UNC1069 / Lazarus Group
actor_profiles.motivation: Financial theft, espionage, supply chain disruption
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Executed $285M theft from Drift Protocol. Compromised Axios npm package maintainer account. Distributed 1,700 malicious packages across multiple registries.

actor_profiles.name: Storm-1175 / Medusa
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Deploying Medusa ransomware within 24 hours of vulnerability disclosure. Chaining zero-day and N-day exploits. Targeting healthcare and education sectors.

actor_profiles.name: APT28 (Forest Blizzard)
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Hijacking SOHO routers for DNS hijacking. Deploying PRISMEX malware. Targeting Ukraine and NATO allies via RTF file exploits.

actor_profiles.name: Iran-Nexus (CyberAv3ngers)
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Compromising US critical infrastructure PLCs. Manipulating SCADA data. Targeting water and energy facilities.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Improper access control vulnerability allowing unauthenticated attackers to bypass API authentication and execute arbitrary code.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply emergency hotfix for EMS 7.4.5/7.4.6 or upgrade to 7.4.7.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Code injection vulnerability in CustomMCP node allowing remote code execution with full Node.js privileges.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Upgrade to Flowise version 3.0.6 or later. Disable CustomMCP node.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Pre-authenticated remote code execution vulnerability in open-source Python notebook.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Upgrade Marimo to the latest patched version immediately.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Critical code injection vulnerability allowing unauthenticated remote code execution.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply latest patch released by Ivanti immediately.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Remote code execution vulnerability in Dawn WebGPU implementation.
Vulnerability allowing attackers to bypass authorization plugins and gain host access.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Monitor for patches; restrict access to Docker API.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Memory overread vulnerability actively probed by attackers.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply available patches from Citrix immediately.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Remote kernel RCE with root shell access.

hunting_leads.title: DPRK LNK File & GitHub C2 Activity
hunting_leads.context: UNC1069 is using LNK files to execute PowerShell scripts that connect to GitHub repositories for C2.
hunting_leads.query: ProcessName='powershell.exe' | where CommandLine contains 'github.com' | where CommandLine contains 'lnk'

hunting_leads.title: Flowise CustomMCP Node Exploitation
hunting_leads.context: Attackers are injecting code into Flowise CustomMCP nodes to achieve RCE.
hunting_leads.query: ProcessName='node' | where CommandLine contains 'CustomMCP' | where CommandLine contains 'Function'
indicators: File system access from Node process

hunting_leads.title: PLC Traffic Anomalies
hunting_leads.context: Iranian actors are manipulating PLCs via specific ports (44818, 2222, 102, 502).
hunting_leads.query: NetworkDestinationPort in (44818, 2222, 102, 502) | where NetworkProtocol == 'TCP' | where SourceIP !in (TrustedOTRange)
indicators: Traffic to OT ports from internet
Connection from overseas IPs to PLCs

hunting_leads.title: Adobe Reader Zero-Day Fingerprinting
hunting_leads.context: Malicious PDFs are exfiltrating system data via RSS.addFeed().
hunting_leads.query: ProcessName='AcroRd32.exe' | where NetworkDestination contains 'rss' | where NetworkDestination contains 'addFeed'
indicators: Adobe Reader connecting to unknown external servers

hunting_leads.title: Trivy Supply Chain Compromise
hunting_leads.context: TeamPCP manipulated Trivy version tags in GitHub Actions to steal credentials.

statistics.key_changes: Exploitation time for critical vulnerabilities has dropped from 61 days to under 24 hours (Marimo, Flowise). AI-driven vulnerability discovery is now outpacing human patching.
emerging_trends: AI agents becoming primary attack surface (Flowise, GrafanaGhost)
State actors targeting OT/PLC for physical disruption
Supply chain attacks targeting developer trust (npm, GitHub Actions)
declining_threats: Traditional phishing (replaced by AI-generated social engineering)
Static malware (replaced by AI-generated polymorphic code)
